1: Moses assembled all the congregation of Israel and said to them, 'These are the things that YHWH has commanded you to do': The transitional verse opening the Tabernacle construction section (Exodus 35-40) begins with Moses assembling kol adat benei Yisrael — the whole congregation, all of Israel. This is the same assembly formula used for major covenant occasions (Exodus 12:3, Exodus 16:1, Exodus 17:1). What follows is not merely construction instructions but the communal execution of a national covenant obligation. The Tabernacle is Israel's covenant project; it belongs to all of them.
2: 'Six days work shall be done, but on the seventh day you shall have a Sabbath of solemn rest, holy to YHWH. Whoever does any work on it shall be put to death': The Sabbath is the first thing Moses communicates after assembling the entire congregation — it is placed before the Tabernacle building instructions for deliberate theological emphasis. Even the sacred construction project cannot override the Sabbath. The phrase Shabbat Shabbaton (Sabbath of solemn rest) places this Sabbath statement in the same intensified register as the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16:31) and the Jubilee year (Leviticus 25:4). The death penalty for violation confirms that the Sabbath is covenantally constitutive.
3: 'You shall kindle no fire in all your dwelling places on the Sabbath day': The fire prohibition is the single specific Sabbath restriction added to this re-statement that was not in the Exodus 31:12-17 version. Its practical implication would include cooking, metalwork, and lighting — the most common daily activities involving fire. The Rabbinic tradition built the entire 39-category system of forbidden Sabbath work (m. Shabbat Exodus 7:2) from the juxtaposition of the Sabbath command here with the Tabernacle construction instructions that follow — the 39 categories are derived from the types of labor used to build the Tabernacle.
4: Moses said to all the congregation of Israel, 'This is the thing that YHWH has commanded: Take from among you a contribution to YHWH. Whoever is of a generous heart, let him bring YHWH's contribution': The Tabernacle is funded by a terumah (H8641, freewill offering/contribution) — a voluntary giving from a generous heart (nadiv lev, H5081+H3820, willing-hearted/generous). Unlike the census ransom (Exodus 30:11-16, a fixed mandatory payment), the Tabernacle construction fund is entirely voluntary. The economic theology is intentional: God's house is built from glad generosity, not legal compulsion.
5: 'Gold, silver, and bronze; blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen, goats' hair': The materials list repeats Exodus 25:3-7 verbatim — this is the execution announcement of the original divine instruction. The three metals (gold, silver, bronze) correspond to the Tabernacle's three zones of holiness. The four fabrics (blue, purple, scarlet, fine linen) are all luxury textiles; blue (tekhelet) and purple (argaman) were Phoenician dyes of exceptional cost, requiring thousands of murex shellfish per gram of dye. The voluntary bringing of these elite materials from Egypt's wealth (the spoils of the exodus) transforms the plunder of an empire into the furnishings of a sanctuary.
6: Tanned rams' skins, goatskins, acacia wood: The leather coverings of the Tabernacle — tanned red-dyed rams' skins and techashim skins (sea cows? dugong? the exact animal is uncertain) — provided the outermost weather protection. Acacia wood (shittim) is the structural material for all the portable wooden elements: the boards, the pillars, the furniture frames. Its density and resistance to insect damage made it ideal for a portable sanctuary.
7: Oil for the light, spices for the anointing oil and for the fragrant incense: The liturgical substances — oil for the lampstand (Exodus 27:20), spices for the anointing oil (Exodus 30:23-25) and incense (Exodus 30:34-38) — are now included in the freewill offering call alongside the structural materials. The Tabernacle requires not only physical construction but ongoing liturgical supply; both are funded by communal generosity.
8: And onyx stones and stones for setting, for the ephod and for the breastpiece: The precious stones for the high priest's garments — the two onyx shoulder stones engraved with tribal names (Exodus 28:9-12) and the twelve breastpiece stones (Exodus 28:15-21) — are the most personally directed of all the materials, belonging to the person of the high priest. Their inclusion in the freewill offering list means that Israel collectively clothes and equips their intercessor before God.
9: Let every skillful craftsman among you come and make all that YHWH has commanded: the tabernacle, its tent and its covering': The call for skilled artisans (chacham-lev, wise/skilled-hearted) now goes to the entire community after the leaders' response (v27). The phrase kol-chakham lev (all who are skilled-hearted) is the same terminology as 31:6 for those whom God filled with skill alongside Bezalel and Oholiab. The community's skilled members are invited to participate in the construction — the Tabernacle is a communal craft project, not solely an elite commission.
10: Its frames, its bars, its pillars, and its bases: The structural elements of the Tabernacle frame — the qerachim (upright frames/planks), the berichim (crossbars), the amudim (pillars), and the adanim (silver socket-bases) — are the portable engineering system that allows a sanctuary of remarkable size (30 cubits by 10 cubits) to be assembled and disassembled in the wilderness. The silver bases (each weighing one talent = ~34 kg) were funded by the census ransom money (Exodus 38:27), creating a literal base of redemption upon which the entire Tabernacle structure rested.
11: The ark and its poles, the mercy seat, and the veil of the screen — the inner core of the Most Holy Place, named first in the construction call to establish the hierarchy of holiness. The items are listed from inside outward (ark, screen/veil) and then the holy place items (table, lampstand, altar of incense) and finally the courtyard elements (altar of burnt offering, laver). The inward-to-outward organization mirrors the priest's movement from periphery to center in service.
12: The ark, its poles, the mercy seat, and the veil of the screen — these are the restricted elements, accessible only to the high priest on Yom Kippur. Their naming in the public construction call acknowledges that though the community builds them, they will never directly interact with them in service. The separation between construction and use is a distinctive feature of the Tabernacle's theology: all of Israel builds what only the high priest may enter.
13: The table, its poles and all its utensils, and the bread of the Presence — the lechem panim (bread of the Presence/face-bread) is the showbread displayed permanently before YHWH, replaced weekly by the priests. It is simultaneously a firstfruits offering and a covenant meal-symbol — twelve loaves for twelve tribes, the entire community present in bread before God.
14: The lampstand for the light, with its utensils and its lamps, and the oil for the light — the menorah and its maintenance supplies. The oil for light (shemen lama'or) is specified separately from the anointing oil, confirming that the lampstand oil was pure olive oil (Exodus 27:20), not the five-spice compound of the anointing oil (Exodus 30:23-25). The permanent lamp's burning through the night was the visible sign of divine attention — the eye of God never closed over his house.
15: The altar of incense and its poles, the anointing oil and the fragrant incense, and the screen for the door at the entrance of the tabernacle — the doorway screen (masakh hapetach) at the tent's entrance performed the same function for the outer door as the veil did for the inner: controlling access and maintaining the holiness gradient from courtyard to holy place.
16: The altar of burnt offering with its grating of bronze, its poles, and all its utensils, the basin and its stand — the courtyard altar and laver are listed last because they are the first things encountered when entering from outside. The bronze grating (michbar nechoshet) of the altar was the metal grid within the altar that held the sacrifices over the fire pit; its bronze material reflects its courtyard (outer zone) placement.
17: The hangings of the court, its pillars and its bases, and the screen for the gate of the court — the courtyard enclosure (100 by 50 cubits, Exodus 27:9-18) was the accessible space for ordinary Israelites; the hanging screen at the courtyard gate marked the first threshold between secular and sacred space. Every Israelite could enter the courtyard; only priests could advance beyond.
18: The pegs of the tabernacle and the pegs of the court, and their cords — the tent pegs (yetedot) and their ropes secured the Tabernacle structure against wind in the wilderness. The mundane hardware — pegs and cords — is included in the list of sacred construction elements, confirming that every component of the Tabernacle, however utilitarian, is consecrated to divine service.
19: The finely worked garments for ministering in the holy place, the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons for their service as priests — the priestly vestments are listed last in the materials call because they are the most personal and status-defining of all the sacred objects. They are made by the skilled artisans (Exodus 28:3) from the community's donated materials; in receiving and wearing them, Aaron and his sons embody the covenant community's entire approach to God.
20: Then all the congregation of Israel departed from the presence of Moses: The assembly dissolves and the people return to their tents to gather their contributions. The absence of verbal response — no question asked, no objection raised — is notable; the community simply goes to act. This silent obedience contrasts with the volatile assembly of Exodus 32; the covenant renewal has produced a different kind of people, at least for this moment.
21: And they came, every one whose heart stirred him (asher nesa'o libo), and every one whose spirit moved him (asher nadeva rucho), and brought YHWH's contribution to be used for the tent of meeting': Two phrases describe the freewill givers: nesa'o libo (whose heart was lifted/stirred) and nadeva rucho (whose spirit was willing/generous). The verb nasa (to lift) applied to the heart (lev) means a disposition elevated toward generosity; nadav (H5068, to be generous/willing) is the root of nedavah (freewill offering). Both phrases describe the motivated, uncoerced impulse to give — the divine Spirit working within the human spirit to produce generosity for the sanctuary.
22: So they came, both men and women. All who were of a willing heart brought brooches and earrings and signet rings and armlets, all sorts of gold objects, every man dedicating an offering of gold to YHWH: The jewelry brought to the Tabernacle is the mirror image of the jewelry stripped and melted for the golden calf (Exodus 32:2-4). The same community that funded the idol now redeems itself by funding the true divine dwelling. The inclusion of women as willing givers alongside men — named separately (anashim al-nashim) — acknowledges that women's wealth (their jewelry) constitutes a significant portion of the Tabernacle's gold supply.
23: And every one who possessed blue or purple or scarlet yarn or fine linen or goats' hair or tanned rams' skins or goatskins brought them: The textile donations come from those who 'possessed' (nimtza ito, was found with him/her) the luxury materials — implying not everyone had them, but those who did brought all of them. The passive possession language (nimtza) may hint at providence: these luxury textiles were in Israel's hands because YHWH had given them favor before the Egyptians (Exodus 12:36).
24: Everyone who could make a contribution of silver or bronze brought it as YHWH's contribution. And every one who possessed acacia wood of any use in the work brought it: The bronze and silver contributions (along with acacia wood) complete the structural materials. Kol asher herim terumas kesef unechoshet (everyone who raised a contribution of silver or bronze) — the verb herim (to lift up) is also the root of terumah (contribution/wave offering), a term that literally means 'lifted/elevated thing.' The giving is enacted linguistically as the same gesture as the ritual elevation of an offering before YHWH.
25: And every skillful woman spun with her hands, and they all brought what they had spun in blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen: The women's spinning is explicitly identified as skilled craft — kol ishah chakhemat-lev (every woman wise-hearted), the same formula used for Bezalel's male artisan companions (Exodus 31:6). The woman-spinners use the same chochmah (wisdom/skill) terminology as the Spirit-filled male craftsmen, and their contribution is structural: without the woven linen and yarns, the Tabernacle's curtains cannot be made. The female workforce of the Tabernacle is invisible in some readings but named and qualified here.
26: All the women whose hearts moved them with skill spun the goats' hair: Goats' hair (izim) spinning is mentioned separately from the fine linen because it is a different technical process — coarser fiber, different twist — and produces the second layer of the Tabernacle's covering. The phrase asher nasa liban otana bechokhmah (whose heart lifted them in skill) is unique — the combination of nasa (lifted/stirred) with chokhmah (wisdom/skill) suggests that artistic skill itself is experienced as a heart-lifting gift, not merely a learned technique.
27: And the leaders (nesi'im) brought onyx stones and stones for setting, for the ephod and for the breastpiece: The leaders (tribal chieftains) bring the most expensive individual items: the gem stones for the high priest's garments and the spices, oil, and lighting oil. The social stratification of giving is preserved — ordinary Israelites give what they have; leaders give what only they can afford. The nesi'im are the same tribal leaders who will lead the Num 1 census; their patronage of the priestly vestments ties tribal leadership to priestly mediation.
28: And spices and oil for the light, and for the anointing oil, and for the fragrant incense: The liturgical substances — the most refined and costly of all the donations — are provided by the leaders. Spices for the anointing oil and incense were imported luxury goods; the fact that Israel possessed them reflects the Egyptian spoils of the exodus. The chain of provision is explicit: Egypt's gold and spices, given to Israel through divine favor, are now consecrated to YHWH's dwelling.
29: All the men and women, the people of Israel, whose heart moved them to bring anything for the work that YHWH had commanded by Moses to be done brought it as a freewill offering (nedavah) to YHWH: The summary verse balances the individual categories with the comprehensive statement: men and women, all Israel, heart-moved, voluntarily. The word nedavah (freewill offering) is the theological signature of the entire construction fund — it is given not because it must be, but because the givers want to. The Tabernacle is thus a monument not only to divine design but to human generosity freely given in response to covenant grace.
30: Then Moses said to the people of Israel, 'See, YHWH has called by name Bezalel the son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah': The re-announcement of Bezalel after the golden-calf interruption (he was first named in Exodus 31:2) signals continuity: the crisis of Exodus 32-34 has not canceled the construction project or the divine appointments. Moses uses the same formula as the first announcement — qara beshem (called by name) — confirming that the divine election is unchanged. The fact that Bezalel is from Judah while the crisis was caused by Levitical Aaron (Exodus 32:1-6) has an implicit contrast: the royal tribe's artisan succeeds where the priestly tribe's leader failed.
31: 'And he has filled him with the Spirit of God, with skill, with intelligence, with knowledge, and with all craftsmanship': The re-statement of Bezalel's Spirit-endowment from 31:3 repeats the three-fold cognitive gift: chokmah (skill/wisdom), tevunah (intelligence/understanding), da'at (knowledge). The context of re-announcement after the golden-calf episode emphasizes that the Spirit's work in artisanal skill is the divine answer to the Spirit-less artisanship of Aaron's calf-making (Exodus 32:4: vayitsarehu bacharet, he fashioned it with an engraving tool — without divine commissioning or Spirit-filling).
32: 'To devise artistic designs, to work in gold and silver and bronze': Lachshov machashavot (to devise artistic plans) — the intellectual-creative dimension of Bezalel's Spirit-given capacity. The divine Spirit enables not only execution but conception; Bezalel thinks artistically before he works artistically. The three metals — gold (inner sanctuary), silver (outer fittings), bronze (courtyard) — represent the entire material range of the Tabernacle, confirming Bezalel's comprehensive mandate.
33: 'In cutting stones for setting, and in carving wood, to work in every skilled craft': The comprehensive scope of Bezalel's craft — gem cutting, woodcarving, metalwork — is re-confirmed. The phrase bechol melechet machashevet (in every work of skilled craft/design) uses melechet (work/craft) with machashevet (design/thought), the same pairing as in Exodus 31:4. The Tabernacle requires both conceptual design and skilled execution; Bezalel carries both gifts.
34: 'And he has inspired him to teach, both him and Oholiab the son of Ahisamach of the tribe of Dan': The teaching gift is a specific addition to the earlier commissioning: velehorot natan belibo (and to teach, he has put in his heart). Bezalel and Oholiab are not only master craftsmen but master teachers — they can transmit their skill to others. This pedagogical gift is essential for a project that requires many workers; the Spirit's endowment for craft includes the ability to multiply that craft through instruction. The paired commissioning (Judah + Dan, south + north) maintains the representative-of-all-Israel character of the artisan leadership.
35: He has filled them with skill to do every sort of work done by an engraver or by a designer or by an embroiderer in blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen, or by a weaver — by any sort of workman or skilled designer': The comprehensive list of artistic specializations — engraving (charash), design/planning (choshev), embroidery (roqem), weaving (orag) — covers the full range of ancient textile and metalwork arts. The divine Spirit's scope of endowment matches the technical scope of the project: whatever the Tabernacle requires, the Spirit equips. The implicit theology is that there is no domain of human skilled work that lies outside the Spirit's capacity to inhabit and empower.
